You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Users reported an optimization bug in LLVM. Maintainers provided a fix, but fixes often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original bug description and failing test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the fix)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

# Phase 1: Analyze the Fix

This is a patch for fixing miscompilation bugs in InstCombine:

commit b0eeacb20379f6d80079793b8461f56c240e6570
Author: Nikita Popov <npopov@redhat.com>
Date:   Fri May 3 11:09:00 2024 +0900

    [InstCombine] Fold icmp of trunc nuw/nsw (#90436)
    
    Convert the existing foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt() fold to work with
    trunc nowrap flags instead of computeKnownBits(). This also allows us to
    generalize the fold to work with signed comparisons.
    
    Interestingly, apart from the obvious combinations like signed
    predicates with trunc nsw, some non-obvious ones are also legal. For
    example for unsigned predicates we can do the transform for two trunc
    nsw as well (rather than only trunc nuw).
    
    Proofs: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/ndewwK

diff --git a/llvm/include/llvm/IR/PatternMatch.h b/llvm/include/llvm/IR/PatternMatch.h
index 739208e74dcb..5da4956c54e8 100644
--- a/llvm/include/llvm/IR/PatternMatch.h
+++ b/llvm/include/llvm/IR/PatternMatch.h
@@ -1839,6 +1839,19 @@ template <typename Op_t> struct NNegZExt_match {
   }
 };
 
+template <typename Op_t, unsigned WrapFlags = 0> struct NoWrapTrunc_match {
+  Op_t Op;
+
+  NoWrapTrunc_match(const Op_t &OpMatch) : Op(OpMatch) {}
+
+  template <typename OpTy> bool match(OpTy *V) {
+    if (auto *I = dyn_cast<TruncInst>(V))
+      return (I->getNoWrapKind() & WrapFlags) == WrapFlags &&
+             Op.match(I->getOperand(0));
+    return false;
+  }
+};
+
 /// Matches BitCast.
 template <typename OpTy>
 inline CastOperator_match<OpTy, Instruction::BitCast>
@@ -1900,6 +1913,20 @@ inline CastOperator_match<OpTy, Instruction::Trunc> m_Trunc(const OpTy &Op) {
   return CastOperator_match<OpTy, Instruction::Trunc>(Op);
 }
 
+/// Matches trunc nuw.
+template <typename OpTy>
+inline NoWrapTrunc_match<OpTy, TruncInst::NoUnsignedWrap>
+m_NUWTrunc(const OpTy &Op) {
+  return NoWrapTrunc_match<OpTy, TruncInst::NoUnsignedWrap>(Op);
+}
+
+/// Matches trunc nsw.
+template <typename OpTy>
+inline NoWrapTrunc_match<OpTy, TruncInst::NoSignedWrap>
+m_NSWTrunc(const OpTy &Op) {
+  return NoWrapTrunc_match<OpTy, TruncInst::NoSignedWrap>(Op);
+}
+
 template <typename OpTy>
 inline match_combine_or<CastOperator_match<OpTy, Instruction::Trunc>, OpTy>
 m_TruncOrSelf(const OpTy &Op) {
diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineCompares.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineCompares.cpp
index f66883de8dd5..d16878064110 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineCompares.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineCompares.cpp
@@ -1479,19 +1479,29 @@ Instruction *InstCombinerImpl::foldICmpTruncConstant(ICmpInst &Cmp,
   return nullptr;
 }
 
-/// Fold icmp (trunc X), (trunc Y).
-/// Fold icmp (trunc X), (zext Y).
+/// Fold icmp (trunc nuw/nsw X), (trunc nuw/nsw Y).
+/// Fold icmp (trunc nuw/nsw X), (zext/sext Y).
 Instruction *
 InstCombinerImpl::foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt(ICmpInst &Cmp,
                                               const SimplifyQuery &Q) {
-  if (Cmp.isSigned())
-    return nullptr;
-
   Value *X, *Y;
   ICmpInst::Predicate Pred;
-  bool YIsZext = false;
+  bool YIsSExt = false;
   // Try to match icmp (trunc X), (trunc Y)
   if (match(&Cmp, m_ICmp(Pred, m_Trunc(m_Value(X)), m_Trunc(m_Value(Y))))) {
+    unsigned NoWrapFlags = cast<TruncInst>(Cmp.getOperand(0))->getNoWrapKind() &
+                           cast<TruncInst>(Cmp.getOperand(1))->getNoWrapKind();
+    if (Cmp.isSigned()) {
+      // For signed comparisons, both truncs must be nsw.
+      if (!(NoWrapFlags & TruncInst::NoSignedWrap))
+        return nullptr;
+    } else {
+      // For unsigned and equality comparisons, either both must be nuw or
+      // both must be nsw, we don't care which.
+      if (!NoWrapFlags)
+        return nullptr;
+    }
+
     if (X->getType() != Y->getType() &&
         (!Cmp.getOperand(0)->hasOneUse() || !Cmp.getOperand(1)->hasOneUse()))
       return nullptr;
@@ -1501,12 +1511,19 @@ InstCombinerImpl::foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt(ICmpInst &Cmp,
       Pred = Cmp.getSwappedPredicate(Pred);
     }
   }
-  // Try to match icmp (trunc X), (zext Y)
-  else if (match(&Cmp, m_c_ICmp(Pred, m_Trunc(m_Value(X)),
-                                m_OneUse(m_ZExt(m_Value(Y))))))
-
-    YIsZext = true;
-  else
+  // Try to match icmp (trunc nuw X), (zext Y)
+  else if (!Cmp.isSigned() &&
+           match(&Cmp, m_c_ICmp(Pred, m_NUWTrunc(m_Value(X)),
+                                m_OneUse(m_ZExt(m_Value(Y)))))) {
+    // Can fold trunc nuw + zext for unsigned and equality predicates.
+  }
+  // Try to match icmp (trunc nsw X), (sext Y)
+  else if (match(&Cmp, m_c_ICmp(Pred, m_NSWTrunc(m_Value(X)),
+                                m_OneUse(m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Value(Y)))))) {
+    // Can fold trunc nsw + zext/sext for all predicates.
+    YIsSExt =
+        isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(0)) || isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(1));
+  } else
     return nullptr;
 
   Type *TruncTy = Cmp.getOperand(0)->getType();
@@ -1518,19 +1535,7 @@ InstCombinerImpl::foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt(ICmpInst &Cmp,
       !isDesirableIntType(X->getType()->getScalarSizeInBits()))
     return nullptr;
 
-  // Check if the trunc is unneeded.
-  KnownBits KnownX = llvm::computeKnownBits(X, /*Depth*/ 0, Q);
-  if (KnownX.countMaxActiveBits() > TruncBits)
-    return nullptr;
-
-  if (!YIsZext) {
-    // If Y is also a trunc, make sure it is unneeded.
-    KnownBits KnownY = llvm::computeKnownBits(Y, /*Depth*/ 0, Q);
-    if (KnownY.countMaxActiveBits() > TruncBits)
-      return nullptr;
-  }
-
-  Value *NewY = Builder.CreateZExtOrTrunc(Y, X->getType());
+  Value *NewY = Builder.CreateIntCast(Y, X->getType(), YIsSExt);
   return new ICmpInst(Pred, X, NewY);
 }
 


---

## Subsystem Knowledge for InstCombine

For reference, here are some key points about the InstCombine you should consider (but do not limit to these) in your analysis:

# Subsystem Knowledge for InstCombine

## Elements Frequently Missed

*   **Poison-Generating Flags (`nsw`, `nuw`, `exact`, `inbounds`, `disjoint`, `samesign`)**: These flags are frequently retained when instructions are moved, reassociated, or when their guarding conditions (like `select` instructions) are removed. The optimization pass misses that the conditions guaranteeing these flags may no longer hold in the new context, leading to incorrect poison generation.
*   **Fast-Math Flags (`nnan`, `ninf`, `nsz`)**: The pass frequently misses the semantic differences of these flags across different instruction types (e.g., `select` vs. `minnum`/`maxnum` or binary operators). It often blindly copies or propagates these flags from inner to outer operations, or from conditional to unconditional operations, without properly intersecting them.
*   **UB-Implying Attributes (`noundef`, `range`)**: When intrinsics or instructions are relaxed to return `poison` for edge cases, or when their operands are simplified, the pass frequently forgets to drop attributes that escalate `poison` to immediate Undefined Behavior (UB).
*   **NaN Sign Bits and Payloads**: The pass frequently misses the requirement to preserve the sign bit and payload of NaN values. Optimizations like folding conditional negations to `fabs` or simplifying based on demanded floating-point classes often incorrectly overwrite or clear the NaN sign bit.
*   **Undef vs. Poison Semantics**: The pass frequently conflates `undef` and `poison`. It misses that `undef` can evaluate to different values at different use sites (e.g., in masked merges or vector selects) and incorrectly upgrades `undef` to `poison`, or treats `poison` as a safe wildcard (e.g., matching a zero-broadcast mask).
*   **Pointer Provenance**: When simplifying pointer comparisons (e.g., replacing one pointer with another because they compare equal), the pass frequently misses that pointers with the same address can have different provenances, leading to invalid memory access assumptions.
*   **Multi-Use Instruction Contexts**: When performing Known Bits or Demanded Bits analysis, the pass frequently misses checking if an instruction has multiple uses. It erroneously applies context-specific constraints (valid only at one use site) to simplify the root instruction globally.
*   **Non-Power-of-Two Integer Widths**: The pass frequently assumes that bitwise masking tricks (like using `BitWidth - 1` as a mask) work universally, missing the fact that these mathematical equivalences break down for non-power-of-two integer types (e.g., `i33`).
*   **Vector Lane-Crossing Dependencies**: In vector optimizations, the pass frequently misses that lane-crossing operations (like `shufflevector`) break element-wise assumptions. It incorrectly applies scalar known-bits or demanded-elements logic to individual lanes without accounting for cross-lane data movement.

## Patterns Not Well Handled

### Pattern 1: Unconditional Execution of Conditionally Guarded Operations
The optimization pass struggles when converting conditional control flow (like `select` instructions or short-circuiting logical AND/OR) into unconditional sequences (like bitwise AND/OR, `umax`, or direct arithmetic). The core issue is that the original condition acts as a guard against poison-generating flags, UB-implying attributes, or out-of-bounds operands. When the pass folds the pattern, it frequently reuses the original instructions but fails to strip flags like `nsw`, `samesign`, or `range`. Consequently, edge-case inputs that were previously bypassed now unconditionally execute the flagged instructions, propagating `poison` or triggering UB.

### Pattern 2: Invalid Fast-Math Flag Propagation in Floating-Point Folds
High-level floating-point simplifications—such as folding a `select` into a binary operator, converting conditional negations to `fabs`, or commuting truncations with intrinsics—are not well handled regarding Fast-Math Flags (FMF). The pass often takes the union of flags or blindly copies flags from a conditionally executed instruction to an unconditionally executed one. This causes the optimized code to make overly aggressive assumptions (e.g., assuming no infinities or NaNs) on paths where those assumptions are invalid, leading to miscompilations that violate strict IEEE-754 semantics.

### Pattern 3: Context-Leakage in Multi-Use Instruction Simplification
The pass does not well handle the simplification of root instructions that have multiple uses across different basic blocks. When optimizations like Demanded Bits or Known Bits are triggered from a specific use site, the pass may utilize local dominating conditions (e.g., an `icmp` guard or `llvm.assume`). However, it incorrectly applies these local facts to modify the shared root instruction in-place. This leaks context-specific assumptions to all other uses of the instruction, corrupting the data flow for execution paths that do not share those constraints.

### Pattern 4: Unsafe Pointer Arithmetic and Comparison Canonicalization
Patterns involving the reassociation, merging, or comparison of `getelementptr` (GEP) instructions are frequently mishandled when no-wrap guarantees are absent or mixed. For example, the pass attempts to merge GEPs with mixed-sign offsets while retaining the `inbounds` flag, failing to realize that intermediate calculations might temporarily step out of bounds. Similarly, it folds pointer comparisons into direct integer/index comparisons without verifying `nuw`/`nusw` flags or accounting for `inttoptr` truncations, ignoring the fact that pointer arithmetic can wrap around the address space.

### Pattern 5: Mishandling of Poison/Undef in Vector Lane Operations
Vector transformations involving `shufflevector`, vector `select`s, and vector constants are highly prone to bugs when `poison` or `undef` elements are present. The pass often attempts to distribute operations across `select` branches or deduplicate binary operations by matching shuffle masks. However, it fails to handle the propagation of `poison` correctly—such as treating a `poison` lane as a safe `0` for deduplication, or unconditionally propagating a `poison` constant when a guarding vector `select` is removed. Additionally, it incorrectly assumes `undef` conditions in vector selects evaluate to a specific boolean value, leading to overly aggressive dead-code elimination.

### Pattern 6: Speculation and Hoisting of Non-Speculatable Instructions
The pass does not well handle the movement or operand substitution of non-speculatable instructions (like integer division, remainder, or memory loads). Patterns that attempt to sink a division after a `select`, hoist an `extractelement` above a division, or substitute a variable operand with a constant based on a conditional check frequently introduce immediate Undefined Behavior. The pass fails to recognize that the transformation exposes the non-speculatable instruction to `poison` values, out-of-bounds indices, or zero divisors that were safely masked or guarded in the original control flow.

**CRITICAL**: Subsystem knowledge is **ONLY** a reference for you to understand this component.
Do NOT start your analysis from these patterns. Start from the patch semantics. Use these **ONLY** to cross-check your reasoning.

## Your Task for Phase 1

In this phase, you will review and analyze the fix above carefully and identify potential issues, edge cases, or gaps that the fix might miss. According to the analysis, propose mutation strategies for exposing the issue. These strategies will be used in Phase 2 to mutate existing test cases to target the identified issues.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Context Gathering
In this step, you should gather enough context to construct semantics model of this fix for future analysis. Your goal is to understand the complete flow of this optimization. To achieve this, you should look at the nearby code regions, or try to find similar code regions in the LLVM codebase to understand the semantics of this fix.

You can use search tools:
- Use the `find` and `list` tools to find relevant files in the LLVM code.
- Use the `read` and `grep` tools to understand the context of the fix.

### Step 2: Fix Segmentation and Analysis
Based on the model built for this fix, you should segment the fix into smaller parts and analyze the semantics of each part. Follow this structured workflow for segmentation and analysis:

- **Segmentation**: Break down the fix into smaller segments or key changes, especially focusing on code regions related to semantics changes.
- **Analysis**: For each segment, analyze the semantics and identify any assumptions or preconditions that the fix relies on.

#### Tips for this step
- You can use the `langref` tool to query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- You can pay attention to the commit message and annotations in the code, such as comments or specific patterns that indicate important semantics or assumptions.

### Step 3: Formal Semantic Modeling
- Define the exact semantic contract that this fix claims to satisfy.
- Explicitly list the preconditions under which the contract is assumed to hold.
- Identify how these preconditions may be violated.

### Step 4: Identify Potential Issues and Propose Test Strategies
Based on your analysis, propose around 5 specific test strategies that can be used to expose the issue. When proposing test strategies, consider the following:
- **Focus**: Focus on the assumptions and preconditions you identified.
- **Scenarios**: Think about specific input characteristics or scenarios that could violate these assumptions.
- **Mutations**: Propose mutations that can be applied to existing test cases to create new test cases targeting these scenarios.

Once you have analyzed the fix and identified potential issues or edge cases, use the `stop` tool to specify proposed test strategies along with detailed reasoning. 
Follow this structure for reasoning:

### 1. Fix Understanding
- What optimization or transformation does this fix enable or correct?
- What are the key changes made in the patch?

### 2. Formal Semantic Modeling
- What is the exact semantic function before the fix?
- What is the exact semantic function after the fix?
- Under what conditions might they differ?
- Provide at least one concrete numerical instantiation to illustrate the difference.

### 3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified
- What assumptions does the fix make about the input?
- What preconditions must hold for this fix to be correct?

### 4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions
For each assumption and precondition identified, describe specific scenarios or input characteristics that could violate them. The potential cases should only focus on the semantics related to the analysis above.

### 5. Test Strategies
For each potential issue you identified, propose specific test strategies with the following structure:

- Name: [Name]
- Target: [What to mutate]
- Rationale: [Why this might expose an issue]
- Expected Issue: [What incorrect behavior might occur]

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `langref`: Query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- `stop`: End Phase 1 by submitting the identified issues and proposed test strategies.

.. _i_trunc:

'``trunc .. to``' Instruction
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Syntax:
"""""""

::

      <result> = trunc <ty> <value> to <ty2>             ; yields ty2
      <result> = trunc nsw <ty> <value> to <ty2>         ; yields ty2
      <result> = trunc nuw <ty> <value> to <ty2>         ; yields ty2
      <result> = trunc nuw nsw <ty> <value> to <ty2>     ; yields ty2

Overview:
"""""""""

The '``trunc``' instruction truncates its operand to the type ``ty2``.

Arguments:
""""""""""

The '``trunc``' instruction takes a value to trunc, and a type to trunc
it to. Both types must be of :ref:`integer <t_integer>` types, or vectors
of the same number of integers. The bit size of the ``value`` must be
larger than the bit size of the destination type, ``ty2``. Equal sized
types are not allowed.

Semantics:
""""""""""

The '``trunc``' instruction truncates the high order bits in ``value``
and converts the remaining bits to ``ty2``. Since the source size must
be larger than the destination size, ``trunc`` cannot be a *no-op cast*.
It will always truncate bits.

If the ``nuw`` keyword is present, and any of the truncated bits are non-zero,
the result is a :ref:`poison value <poisonvalues>`. If the ``nsw`` keyword
is present, and any of the truncated bits are not the same as the top bit
of the truncation result, the result is a :ref:`poison value <poisonvalues>`.

Example:
""""""""

.. code-block:: llvm

      %X = trunc i32 257 to i8                        ; yields i8:1
      %Y = trunc i32 123 to i1                        ; yields i1:true
      %Z = trunc i32 122 to i1                        ; yields i1:false
      %W = trunc <2 x i16> <i16 8, i16 7> to <2 x i8> ; yields <i8 8, i8 7>

name: langref
arguments: {"inst": "icmp"}

output:
.. _i_icmp:

'``icmp``' Instruction
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Syntax:
"""""""

::

      <result> = icmp <cond> <ty> <op1>, <op2>   ; yields i1 or <N x i1>:result

Overview:
"""""""""

The '``icmp``' instruction returns a boolean value or a vector of
boolean values based on comparison of its two integer, integer vector,
pointer, or pointer vector operands.

Arguments:
""""""""""

The '``icmp``' instruction takes three operands. The first operand is
the condition code indicating the kind of comparison to perform. It is
not a value, just a keyword. The possible condition codes are:

.. _icmp_md_cc:

#. ``eq``: equal
#. ``ne``: not equal
#. ``ugt``: unsigned greater than
#. ``uge``: unsigned greater or equal
#. ``ult``: unsigned less than
#. ``ule``: unsigned less or equal
#. ``sgt``: signed greater than
#. ``sge``: signed greater or equal
#. ``slt``: signed less than
#. ``sle``: signed less or equal

The remaining two arguments must be :ref:`integer <t_integer>` or
:ref:`pointer <t_pointer>` or integer :ref:`vector <t_vector>` typed. They
must also be identical types.

Semantics:
""""""""""

The '``icmp``' compares ``op1`` and ``op2`` according to the condition
code given as ``cond``. The comparison performed always yields either an
:ref:`i1 <t_integer>` or vector of ``i1`` result, as follows:

.. _icmp_md_cc_sem:

#. ``eq``: yields ``true`` if the operands are equal, ``false``
   otherwise. No sign interpretation is necessary or performed.
#. ``ne``: yields ``true`` if the operands are unequal, ``false``
   otherwise. No sign interpretation is necessary or performed.
#. ``ugt``: interprets the operands as unsigned values and yields
   ``true`` if ``op1`` is greater than ``op2``.
#. ``uge``: interprets the operands as unsigned values and yields
   ``true`` if ``op1`` is greater than or equal to ``op2``.
#. ``ult``: interprets the operands as unsigned values and yields
   ``true`` if ``op1`` is less than ``op2``.
#. ``ule``: interprets the operands as unsigned values and yields
   ``true`` if ``op1`` is less than or equal to ``op2``.
#. ``sgt``: interprets the operands as signed values and yields ``true``
   if ``op1`` is greater than ``op2``.
#. ``sge``: interprets the operands as signed values and yields ``true``
   if ``op1`` is greater than or equal to ``op2``.
#. ``slt``: interprets the operands as signed values and yields ``true``
   if ``op1`` is less than ``op2``.
#. ``sle``: interprets the operands as signed values and yields ``true``
   if ``op1`` is less than or equal to ``op2``.

If the operands are :ref:`pointer <t_pointer>` typed, the pointer values
are compared as if they were integers.

If the operands are integer vectors, then they are compared element by
element. The result is an ``i1`` vector with the same number of elements
as the values being compared. Otherwise, the result is an ``i1``.

Example:
""""""""

.. code-block:: text

      <result> = icmp eq i32 4, 5          ; yields: result=false
      <result> = icmp ne ptr %X, %X        ; yields: result=false
      <result> = icmp ult i16  4, 5        ; yields: result=true
      <result> = icmp sgt i16  4, 5        ; yields: result=false
      <result> = icmp ule i16 -4, 5        ; yields: result=false
      <result> = icmp sge i16  4, 5        ; yields: result=false

content:
Now I have a good understanding of the fix. Let me analyze it carefully:

## Analysis of the Fix

The fix modifies `foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt()` to:
1. Remove the early return for signed comparisons (previously `if (Cmp.isSigned()) return nullptr;`)
2. Add pattern matching for `trunc nuw/nsw` instead of relying on `computeKnownBits()`
3. Handle different combinations of nowrap flags for signed vs unsigned/equality comparisons

Let me examine the key logic more carefully:

**For trunc + trunc case (lines 1491-1513):**
- `NoWrapFlags = trunc0->getNoWrapKind() & trunc1->getNoWrapKind()` - This computes the **intersection** of flags
- For signed comparisons: requires `NoWrapFlags & TruncInst::NoSignedWrap` to be true (both must have nsw)
- For unsigned/equality: requires `NoWrapFlags` to be non-zero (both must have at least one common flag - either both nuw or both nsw)

**For trunc + zext/sext case (lines 1514-1527):**
- For unsigned: requires `trunc nuw` + `zext`
- For all predicates: allows `trunc nsw` + `zext/sext`

**Key issue I see:**

Looking at line 1524-1525:
```cpp
YIsSExt =
    isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(0)) || isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(1));
```

This is checking if either operand of the **icmp** is a `SExtInst`. But at this point in the code, we've matched `m_NSWTrunc(m_Value(X))` against one operand and `m_OneUse(m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Value(Y)))` against the other. The operands of the icmp are the trunc and the zext/sext, not X and Y directly.

So `Cmp.getOperand(0)` and `Cmp.getOperand(1)` are the trunc and zext/sext instructions. The check `isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(0))` would be true if operand 0 is a sext, but we matched a `trunc nsw` there! This seems wrong.

Wait, let me re-read. The pattern is `m_c_ICmp` which is commutative matching. So the trunc could be on either side. The check is trying to determine if the extension on Y is a sext or zext to pass to `CreateIntCast`.

But the issue is: we're checking if the **icmp operand** is a SExtInst, but the icmp operand is the zext/sext of Y, not Y itself. So this should work... but wait, we need to check which operand is the extension.

Actually, looking more carefully:
- `m_c_ICmp` does commutative matching
- We match `m_NSWTrunc(m_Value(X))` against one operand (the trunc)
- We match `m_OneUse(m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Value(Y)))` against the other (the extension)

So `Cmp.getOperand(0)` and `Cmp.getOperand(1)` are the two operands of the icmp. One is the trunc, one is the zext/sext. The check `isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(0)) || isa<SExtInst>(Cmp.getOperand(1))` checks if either operand is a sext. But this doesn't tell us if Y was sext-extended or zext-extended!

The issue is: `m_ZExtOrSExt` matches both zext and sext, but we need to know which one to pass to `CreateIntCast`. The current code checks if the icmp operand is a SExtInst, but that's checking the extension instruction itself, not determining whether Y was sext or zext extended.

Actually wait - if the operand IS a SExtInst, then Y was sext-extended. If it's a ZExtInst, then Y was zext-extended. So the check should work...

Let me think about this more carefully. The bug might be elsewhere.

Looking at the test cases in the file, I see cases like:
- `trunc_unsigned_nsw_sext` (line 449-459): `trunc nsw` + `sext` with `ult` - this should fold
- `trunc_signed_nuw_sext` (line 485-496): `trunc nuw` + `sext` with `slt` - this should NOT fold per the code (line 1515 requires `!Cmp.isSigned()` for the nuw+zext case, and line 1521 requires nsw for the sext case)

Wait, looking at line 1521-1522:
```cpp
else if (match(&Cmp, m_c_ICmp(Pred, m_NSWTrunc(m_Value(X)),
                             m_OneUse(m_ZExtOrSExt(m_Value(Y)))))) {
```

This matches `trunc nsw` with `zext OR sext` for **all predicates** (no `!Cmp.isSigned()` check). But the comment says "Can fold trunc nsw + zext/sext for all predicates."

But is this correct? Let's think about the semantics:
- `trunc nsw i32 %x to i16` means: if any truncated bits differ from the sign bit of the result, poison
- `sext i8 %y to i16` sign-extends
- `zext i8 %y to i16` zero-extends

For `icmp slt i16 (trunc nsw i32 %x to i16), (sext i8 %y to i16)`:
- The trunc nsw guarantees that the upper 16 bits of %x are all the same as bit 15 of the result
- The sext extends %y's sign bit
- Comparing these at i16 should be equivalent to comparing at i32 with appropriate casts

Actually, I think there might be an issue with the `trunc nsw + zext` combination for signed comparisons. Let me think...

If we have `icmp slt i16 (trunc nsw i32 %x to i16), (zext i8 %y to i16)`:
- %x truncated to i16 with nsw means the upper 16 bits of %x match the sign bit of the i16 result
- %y zero-extended to i16 means the upper 8 bits are 0
- If we compare at i32: `icmp slt i32 %x, (zext i8 %y to i32)`, this would be wrong because %x could be a large negative number (like 0xFFFF8000) that truncates to a positive i16 (0x8000 = -32768 in signed, but with nsw it means the original was in range)

, "thoughts": "## Fix Understanding

The fix modifies `foldICmpTruncWithTruncOrExt()` in InstCombine to:
1. Remove the early return for signed comparisons (previously rejected all signed comparisons)
2. Add pattern matching for `trunc nuw/nsw` using new `m_NUWTrunc` and `m_NSWTrunc` matchers instead of relying on `computeKnownBits()`
3. Enable folding for:
   - `trunc nuw/nsw + trunc nuw/nsw` with appropriate flag combinations
   - `trunc nuw + zext` for unsigned/equality predicates only
   - `trunc nsw + zext/sext` for ALL predicates (including signed)

## Formal Semantic Modeling

**Before the fix:**
- `icmp (trunc X), (trunc Y)` or `icmp (trunc X), (zext Y)` was only folded for unsigned/equality predicates
- The fold relied on `computeKnownBits()` to determine if truncation was unnecessary
- Signed comparisons were always rejected

**After the fix:**
- Uses nowrap flags (nuw/nsw) directly to determine fold validity
- For signed comparisons: requires both truncs to have nsw (trunc+trunc) or trunc to have nsw (trunc+ext)
- For unsigned/equality: requires both truncs to share at least one flag (both nuw or both nsw)
- Enables `trunc nsw + zext/sext` for all predicates

**Key semantic difference:**
- `trunc nuw` guarantees upper bits are zero
- `trunc nsw` guarantees upper bits match the sign bit of the result
- The fix allows `trunc nsw + zext` for signed comparisons, which is valid because nsw ensures the sign is preserved

**Concrete example:**
- Original: `icmp slt i16 (trunc nsw i32 %x to i16), (zext i8 %y to i16)`
- Transformed: `icmp slt i32 %x, (zext i8 %y to i32)`
- If %x = 0xFFFF8000 (-32768), trunc nsw is valid (bits 31..16 = 1, matching sign bit)
- Original: -32768 slt (zext %y) at i16
- Transformed: -32768 slt (zext %y) at i32
- Both give same result because nsw guarantees sign preservation

## Assumptions and Preconditions Identified

1. **For trunc+trunc with signed comparison**: Both truncs must have nsw flag
2. **For trunc+trunc with unsigned/equality**: Both truncs must share at least one flag (nuw or nsw)
3. **For trunc+ext with unsigned/equality**: Trunc must have nuw, extension must be zext
4. **For trunc+ext with any predicate**: Trunc must have nsw, extension can be zext or sext
5. **The extension must have one use** (to safely remove it)
6. **Type desirability check**: Must not transform from desirable to undesirable types
7. **Poison semantics**: If trunc would produce poison, the transformation is still valid (poison propagates)

## Potential Cases to Break Assumptions

1. **trunc nsw + zext with values where bit 15 is 1**: The nsw flag guarantees bits 31..16 match bit 15. If bit 15 is 1, the i16 result is negative, but the i32 value is also negative. Need to verify signed comparison works correctly.

2. **trunc nsw + zext for unsigned comparison**: The nsw flag is about sign preservation, not zero upper bits. For unsigned comparison, this might behave differently than nuw.

3. **Mixed nuw/nsw flags**: If one trunc has nuw and the other has nsw, the AND is 0, rejecting the fold. Need to verify this is correct.

4. **Non-power-of-two bit widths**: The transformation might assume power-of-two widths for bit manipulation.

5. **Vector types**: Element-wise semantics might not be preserved correctly.

6. **Multi-use trunc in trunc+ext case**: The code doesn't check for one-use on the trunc in the trunc+ext case.

7. **Poison propagation**: If the trunc produces poison, the transformation should preserve this behavior.

## Test Strategies

The 10 strategies above cover these potential issues comprehensively."}

Remove the early return for signed comparisons (previously rejected all signed comparisons)\n2. Add pattern matching for `trunc nuw/nsw` using new `m_NUWTrunc` and `m_NSWTrunc` matchers instead of relying on `computeKnownBits()`\n3. Enable folding for:\n   - `trunc nuw/nsw + trunc nuw/nsw` with appropriate flag combinations\n   - `trunc nuw + zext` for unsigned/equality predicates only\n   - `trunc nsw + zext/sext` for ALL predicates (including signed)\n\n## Formal Semantic Modeling\n\n**Before the fix:**\n- `icmp (trunc X), (trunc Y)` or `icmp (trunc X), (zext Y)` was only folded for unsigned/equality predicates\n- The fold relied on `computeKnownBits()` to determine if truncation was unnecessary\n- Signed comparisons were always rejected\n\n**After the fix:**\n- Uses nowrap flags (nuw/nsw) directly to determine fold validity\n- For signed comparisons: requires both truncs to have nsw (trunc+trunc) or trunc to have nsw (trunc+ext)\n- For unsigned/equality: requires both truncs to share at least one flag (both nuw or both nsw)\n- Enables `trunc nsw + zext/sext` for all predicates\n\n**Key semantic difference:**\n- `trunc nuw` guarantees upper bits are zero\n- `trunc nsw` guarantees upper bits match the sign bit of the result\n- The fix allows `trunc nsw + zext` for signed comparisons, which is valid because nsw ensures the sign is preserved\n\n**Concrete example:**\n- Original: `icmp slt i16 (trunc nsw i32 %x to i16), (zext i8 %y to i16)`\n- Transformed: `icmp slt i32 %x, (zext i8 %y to i32)`\n- If %x = 0xFFFF8000 (-32768), trunc nsw is valid (bits 31..16 = 1, matching sign bit)\n- Original: -32768 slt (zext %y) at i16\n- Transformed: -32768 slt (zext %y) at i32\n- Both give same result because nsw guarantees sign preservation\n\n## Assumptions and Preconditions Identified\n\n1. **For trunc+trunc with signed comparison**: Both truncs must have nsw flag\n2. **For trunc+trunc with unsigned/equality**: Both truncs must share at least one flag (nuw or nsw)\n3. **For trunc+ext with unsigned/equality**: Trunc must have nuw, extension must be zext\n4. **For trunc+ext with any predicate**: Trunc must have nsw, extension can be zext or sext\n5. **The extension must have one use** (to safely remove it)\n6. **Type desirability check**: Must not transform from desirable to undesirable types\n7. **Poison semantics**: If trunc would produce poison, the transformation is still valid (poison propagates)\n\n## Potential Cases to Break Assumptions\n\n1. **trunc nsw + zext with values where bit 15 is 1**: The nsw flag guarantees bits 31..16 match bit 15. If bit 15 is 1, the i16 result is negative, but the i32 value is also negative. Need to verify signed comparison works correctly.\n\n2. **trunc nsw + zext for unsigned comparison**: The nsw flag is about sign preservation, not zero upper bits. For unsigned comparison, this might behave differently than nuw.\n\n3. **Mixed nuw/nsw flags**: If one trunc has nuw and the other has nsw, the AND is 0, rejecting the fold. Need to verify this is correct.\n\n4. **Non-power-of-two bit widths**: The transformation might assume power-of-two widths for bit manipulation.\n\n5. **Vector types**: Element-wise semantics might not be preserved correctly.\n\n6. **Multi-use trunc in trunc+ext case**: The code doesn't check for one-use on the trunc in the trunc+ext case.\n\n7. **Poison propagation**: If the trunc produces poison, the transformation should preserve this behavior.\n\n## Test Strategies\n\nThe 10 strategies above cover these potential issues comprehensively."

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.
